content.text: Enabling Cowork in the Desktop App

Claude Cowork is accessed primarily through a dedicated desktop application for macOS and Windows. Unlike the Claude.ai web interface, the desktop app provides persistent background execution, system-level permission grants (file access, notifications), and a local WebSocket relay that enables connectors to reach tools installed on your machine. This guide covers the full installation pipeline — downloading, platform-specific setup, signing in, and flipping on Cowork mode — plus how to verify everything is wired correctly before you start your first Project.

attrs.alt: Enabling Cowork in Desktop: Setup Flow — four-step horizontal pipeline with macOS and Windows installation panels below

content.text: The four top-level steps are the same on both platforms: download the installer, run the platform-specific setup, sign in with your Anthropic account, then flip the Cowork toggle in Settings. The platform differences appear in steps 2 and 3 — specifically, how each OS handles installer trust and which permission dialogs appear.
Anthropic account with a qualifying plan.
Cowork features are available on Pro (individual), Team, and Enterprise plans. A free Claude account will not surface the Cowork toggle in Settings even after installing the desktop app.
macOS 12 Monterey or later; Windows 10 version 22H2 or later. Linux users must access Cowork via the browser — there is no Linux desktop app.
The OAuth flow, model calls, and connector sync all require outbound HTTPS to api.anthropic.com and *.cowork.anthropic.com. Firewalled networks need those domains allowlisted.

The installer places the app in the user-local application directory by default, so administrator rights are not required. An IT-managed device may have restrictions — confirm with your IT team before installing on a corporate machine.

The desktop app is distributed from the official Claude download page. Always download from there rather than third-party mirrors — the app is signed with Anthropic's developer certificate, and installing an unsigned copy from another source will fail the platform's code-signing check.
Open your browser and navigate to the Claude download page.
Click "Download for Mac" or "Download for Windows" depending on your platform. The page auto-detects your OS and highlights the correct button.
Wait for the download to complete. The macOS file is a disk image (
Opening the DMG and Copying the App
file. macOS mounts it as a virtual disk and opens a Finder window showing the Claude Cowork app icon and an /Applications shortcut.
Drag the Claude Cowork icon onto the /Applications shortcut. The OS copies the application bundle to your Applications folder.
Eject the disk image (drag the mounted volume to the Trash, or right-click → Eject in Finder's sidebar).

macOS Gatekeeper quarantines apps downloaded from the internet. On the first launch it will show a dialog: "Claude Cowork cannot be opened because it is from an unidentified developer" even though the app is correctly signed — this happens because Gatekeeper requires you to explicitly approve each new application.
double-click the app icon in the first attempt. Instead:
Right-click (or Control-click) the Claude Cowork icon in Applications.
In the Gatekeeper dialog that now appears, click "Open" to confirm. macOS records your approval and will not prompt again for this app on subsequent launches.
On macOS 13+, go to System Settings → Privacy & Security and look for the "Claude Cowork was blocked" notice at the bottom of the page. Click "Allow Anyway," then open the app normally.
The app requests several system permissions on first launch. Denying any of them degrades functionality in specific ways:
Scheduled task completion alerts, connector sync errors
Tasks run silently — no alerts when a scheduled task finishes or fails
Grants Claude access to local files when you share them in a Project
File sharing from the file picker will fail with a permission error
Global keyboard shortcut to open Cowork from any app
Shortcut won't work; must switch to the app manually
You can review and change these at any time under System Settings → Privacy & Security → the relevant category.
. Windows SmartScreen may show a blue warning dialog because the file was downloaded from the internet.
Click "More info" at the bottom of the SmartScreen dialog, then "Run anyway". This one-time step tells Windows you trust the signed Anthropic installer.
If User Account Control (UAC) prompts, click Yes. The installer places the app under
by default — a per-user install that does not require admin rights. Alternatively, an IT-provided MSI can install machine-wide under
The installer completes and launches the app automatically.

On first launch, Windows Firewall may prompt about allowing Claude Cowork to communicate on the network. The app uses a local WebSocket connection (localhost, not the internet) to relay messages between the connector runtime and the Claude cloud. Click "Allow access" to permit this — denying it prevents connectors from functioning even for operations that only touch your local machine.
Connectors fail; app shows "offline" for local tools
No task completion or error alerts from the system tray
Task Manager → Startup tab, or the app's own Settings → General → Launch at startup
Scheduled tasks can't run while the app isn't open

Authentication uses OAuth 2.0 with PKCE. The desktop app generates a one-time authorization URL and opens it in your system's default browser. You approve the login in the browser, Anthropic's authorization server redirects back to a
custom protocol URL, and the desktop app intercepts that redirect to receive the access and refresh tokens. No password is ever entered inside the app itself.
Click "Sign in with Anthropic" in the app's welcome screen. Your default browser opens the Anthropic sign-in page.
If you already have a Claude session active in that browser, you'll be asked to confirm — click "Authorize" to grant the desktop app access.
If not signed in, complete the standard Anthropic sign-in (email + password or SSO, depending on your plan).
After you approve, the browser shows a "Return to Claude Cowork" confirmation, and the desktop app receives the auth tokens and shows your account name.
If the browser stays on the confirmation page and the app doesn't advance, the
protocol may not be registered. On macOS, re-open the app (it registers the handler on launch). On Windows, run the installer again — it registers the protocol handler at install time.

Enterprise accounts configured with SAML SSO follow the same flow, but the Anthropic sign-in page redirects to your identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace SAML, etc.) before returning. The app treats this transparently — you just complete the SSO prompt in the browser. If your IdP enforces device compliance or certificate authentication, the SSO flow may not complete until your machine passes the policy check.

Signing in loads the standard Claude chat interface. Cowork features (Projects, scheduled tasks, connectors) live behind a separate toggle because they require server-side provisioning against your plan. The toggle is only visible if your account is on a qualifying plan.
Click the app name ("Claude Cowork") in the macOS menu bar.
Flip the "Enable Cowork" toggle to On. The app restarts its backend session and the Projects sidebar appears.
Click File in the menu bar, then Settings.
Navigate to the Cowork section in the left panel.
If the Cowork section is absent from Settings, the account logged in does not have a qualifying plan. Log out, then log in with the correct account or upgrade the plan before returning.
Once Cowork mode is on, the desktop app's interface gains several panels not present in standard Claude:
List of your Cowork Projects with persistent memory and connector configs
Add OAuth grants for Drive, Slack, GitHub, Jira, etc.
Define recurring or one-shot tasks Claude executes automatically
Audit trail of all agent actions, tool calls, and connector reads
Shows child agent progress in real time; lets you pause or cancel
Run these checks before setting up any connectors or scheduled tasks, so you have a known-good baseline:
Your name and plan badge (Pro / Team / Enterprise) should appear in the top-right corner of the app.
The Projects sidebar should be visible. If it is not, open Settings → Cowork and verify the toggle is On.
Create a new Project, type a simple question, and verify Claude responds. This confirms the API connection is live.

In Settings → Connectors, the header should show a green "Runtime connected" indicator. An orange or red indicator means the local WebSocket relay did not start — usually a Firewall block on Windows or a port conflict on macOS.
Schedule a one-time task that runs in two minutes and verify you receive a system notification when it completes.
The connector runtime listens on a localhost port (typically 49152–49199). If another process holds that port range, the runtime fails silently. You can check from a terminal:

# macOS / Linux
lsof -i :49152-49199

# Windows (PowerShell)
Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 49152..49199 | Select-Object LocalPort,State,OwningProcess
If you see an entry for
in the output, the runtime is running. If you see a different process holding the port, either quit that process or restart the app so it picks a different port from the dynamic range.
"Cannot be opened" dialog on macOS even after right-click → Open
Account not on a qualifying plan; check Plan in Settings
Browser opens sign-in but app never advances
macOS: relaunch app; Windows: re-run installer
Connector runtime shows orange / red status
Port blocked by firewall or held by another process
Allow app through firewall; check port range with lsof / Get-NetTCPConnection
No system notifications even after enabling in OS settings
App-level notification permission denied at OS prompt
macOS: System Settings → Notifications → Claude Cowork → Allow. Windows: Settings → System → Notifications
"Damaged and can't be opened" on macOS
Extended attributes from quarantine not cleared properly
Browser has stale SSO cookies from an earlier session
Clear cookies for anthropic.com and your IdP domain, then retry sign-in
App installs but Windows reports virus warning
Antivirus heuristic false positive on new app build
Verify file hash from official release notes; add antivirus exclusion for install dir

The desktop app supports one signed-in account per running instance. If you need to work across multiple Anthropic accounts (for example, a personal Pro account and a Team account for your employer), you have two options:
Settings → Account → Sign out, then sign in with the other account. Switching takes about 10 seconds and preserves Project metadata (reconnects to whichever Projects that account owns).
Use the web app in parallel.

The web interface at claude.ai supports Cowork features if your plan qualifies, and you can be signed into different accounts in different browsers simultaneously. Connector functionality is limited on web compared to desktop (no local file grants).
Claude Cowork auto-updates in the background on both platforms. The update cycle works as follows:
The app checks for a new version at startup and periodically while running.
When an update is available, it downloads silently in the background.
A banner appears offering to restart and apply the update. You can defer it until a convenient time.
To check the installed version or force an update check: Help → About Claude Cowork (macOS) or Help → Check for Updates (Windows).
In managed enterprise environments, the auto-update endpoint may be blocked by policy. Your IT team can distribute updates via the MSI installer or a software management platform.
Enabling Cowork via the Web Interface
If you cannot install the desktop app (Linux, IT-restricted device), Cowork is partially available through the browser at claude.ai. The sign-in flow is the same OAuth sequence. After signing in:
Navigate to the Cowork section and enable it.
Projects, scheduled tasks, and cloud-hosted connectors (Drive, Slack, GitHub, Jira) function normally. Local file grants and the connector runtime are not available from the browser.
Why Desktop vs Browser Matters for Cowork
Several Cowork features depend on capabilities that browsers do not expose:
Background task execution when app minimized
No (tasks pause if tab is closed)

Uninstalling removes the application binary but does not delete your Projects or connector configurations — those are stored server-side and persist on your Anthropic account. You will retain them if you reinstall later.
Drag Claude Cowork from /Applications to Trash. To also remove local app data:
Settings → Apps → Installed apps → Claude Cowork → Uninstall. Local data at
is removed automatically by the uninstaller.

Corporate networks that route traffic through an HTTP or HTTPS proxy require additional configuration. The desktop app reads system proxy settings automatically on both platforms, but this does not always cover the WebSocket connection used by the connector runtime.

Set your proxy in System Settings → Network → (your connection) → Details → Proxies. Claude Cowork inherits these OS-level settings for all HTTPS calls. If your proxy requires authentication (username + password), ensure macOS Keychain has the credentials stored so the app can pass them silently.

Claude Cowork uses the WinInet proxy stack, which respects the settings in Windows Settings → Network & Internet → Proxy. If your organization uses a PAC file, ensure it is accessible from the device. If direct fallback is disabled in the PAC file, the connector runtime WebSocket connection must also go through the proxy — verify the proxy supports WebSocket upgrades (HTTP CONNECT tunneling for port 443).

# Check current WinInet proxy setting from PowerShell
$proxy = Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings'
Write-Output "Proxy enabled: $($proxy.ProxyEnable)"
Write-Output "Proxy server: $($proxy.ProxyServer)"
Write-Output "PAC URL: $($proxy.AutoConfigURL)"
If outbound traffic is filtered at a network gateway rather than through a client-side proxy, allowlist these domains for the app to function fully:
Project sync, scheduled task orchestration, connector relay

Scheduled tasks can only run while the desktop app process is alive. If you use scheduled tasks (Team and Enterprise plans), configuring the app to start at login ensures tasks don't silently miss their windows when the machine reboots.
Enable "Launch at login". On macOS 13+, this adds a Background Allow entry under System Settings → General → Login Items. You can verify or remove it there.
Optionally enable "Start minimized" so the app loads in the menu bar without bringing a window to front at login.
Open File → Settings → General in Claude Cowork.
Enable "Launch at startup". This writes a Run key to the registry under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run.
If group policy prevents startup entries, your IT team can deploy the registry key via GPO or configure startup through a managed software tool.

When no task is actively running, Claude Cowork sits idle at under 1% CPU and roughly 80–120 MB RAM. The connector runtime poll interval is configurable (Settings → Connectors → Sync interval), defaulting to 5 minutes for Drive and Jira connectors. Reducing this to 1 minute increases sync freshness at the cost of more frequent API calls; increasing it to 30 minutes is appropriate for low-activity Projects.

Enabling Cowork follows a four-step pattern on both macOS and Windows: download the signed installer, complete the platform-specific installation (including Gatekeeper bypass on macOS or SmartScreen on Windows), sign in via the OAuth browser flow, and flip the Cowork toggle in Settings. Platform permissions — notifications for scheduled task alerts, files & folders for local file grants, and network access for the connector runtime — are what give the desktop app capabilities the browser cannot replicate. Configure startup-at-login if you rely on scheduled tasks, and allowlist the required Anthropic domains on proxied or firewalled networks. Verify each layer (API connectivity, connector runtime status, notification delivery) before configuring any Project-level connectors or scheduled tasks, so troubleshooting stays isolated to one component at a time.
